series.title: [PATCH] accel/mshv: skip double unmap of mem regions

review.summary: The fix addresses the reported double-unmap regression in principle, but the implementation is not safe as merged. The new tracking table keys `hwaddr` through `GINT_TO_POINTER()`, which truncates high guest physical addresses on 64-bit machines and can cause incorrect map/unmap suppression for regions above 4 GiB. There is also a listener-lifetime leak because the newly allocated hash table is never destroyed.

stages.A: Intent is clear and matches the regression description: track currently mapped regions so ROM-device transitions do not issue duplicate unmaps after the dynamic mapping handler removal.
stages.B: Correctness is not sufficient. The chosen key representation is not 64-bit safe for `hwaddr`, so the table can alias distinct guest physical addresses and silently skip required updates.
stages.C: Regression risk is high for guests that place RAM/ROM or pflash above 4 GiB, because map state can be corrupted by address truncation. There is also a smaller teardown leak from the missing hash-table destruction.
stages.D: Validation is incomplete. The patch should be exercised with ROM-device / OVMF mappings above 4 GiB and with listener teardown paths to confirm both functional behavior and lifecycle cleanup.
stages.E: Not ready to merge as-is. Use a 64-bit-safe keying strategy for `mapped_regions` and add corresponding cleanup when the memory listener is unregistered or destroyed.

findings.title: `hwaddr` is truncated when used as a hash-table key
findings.description: The tracking table stores `start_addr` via `GINT_TO_POINTER()`. `hwaddr` is 64-bit on common QEMU targets, while `GINT_TO_POINTER()` only preserves an integer-sized GLib `gint` payload. Guest physical addresses above 32 bits will alias in `mapped_regions`, so `uaddr_is_mapped()` can return true for the wrong region and suppress required map/unmap operations. This directly undermines the intended fix for OVMF and is especially risky because firmware and RAM are frequently placed above 4 GiB.
patch_context: +static void track_region(MshvMemoryListener *mml, hwaddr start_addr, bool add)
findings.suggestion: Do not encode `hwaddr` with `GINT_TO_POINTER()`. Use a 64-bit-safe key, for example a hash table created with `g_int64_hash`/`g_int64_equal` and heap-allocated `uint64_t` keys, or another QEMU/GLib container that preserves full `hwaddr` width.
findings.confidence_reason: This is a direct type-width mismatch visible in the patch. `hwaddr` is wider than the GLib integer-to-pointer helper used here, and the failure mode is straightforward key aliasing.

findings.title: New `mapped_regions` table is allocated but never freed
findings.description: `register_mshv_memory_listener()` now allocates a `GHashTable` for every `MshvMemoryListener`, but the patch does not add any corresponding destruction in the listener unregister or accelerator teardown path. Even if this is not fatal during a normal process lifetime, it is still a resource leak introduced by the change and should be fixed alongside the new state.
findings.suggestion: Destroy `mml->mapped_regions` in the matching unregister/destruction path and null the field after free. If the keying strategy changes to allocated 64-bit keys, ensure the hash table is created with appropriate key destroy notifications as well.
findings.confidence_reason: The allocation is explicit in the patch and no matching cleanup is shown. The exact teardown function is outside the provided diff, but the new ownership introduced here still needs a corresponding free path.

disclaimer: LLM-generated draft. Not an authoritative review.
